@@ -0,0 +1,445 @@
+# KEP-349: All-or-nothing semantics for workload resource assignment
+
+<!--
+This is the title of your KEP. Keep it short, simple, and descriptive. A good
+title can help communicate what the KEP is and should be considered as part of
+any review.
+-->
+
+<!--
+A table of contents is helpful for quickly jumping to sections of a KEP and for
+highlighting any additional information provided beyond the standard KEP
+template.
+
+Ensure the TOC is wrapped with
+  <code>&lt;!-- toc --&rt;&lt;!-- /toc --&rt;</code>
+tags, and then generate with `hack/update-toc.sh`.
+-->
+
+<!-- toc -->
+- [Summary](#summary)
+- [Motivation](#motivation)
+  - [Goals](#goals)
+  - [Non-Goals](#non-goals)
+- [Proposal](#proposal)
+  - [User Stories (Optional)](#user-stories-optional)
+    - [Story 1](#story-1)
+    - [Story 2](#story-2)
+  - [Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)](#notescontraintscaveats-optional)
+  - [Risks and Mitigations](#risks-and-mitigations)
+- [Design Details](#design-details)
+  - [PodsReady workload condition](#podsready-workload-condition)
+  - [Workload API](#workload-api)
+  - [Job annotation](#job-annotation)
+  - [The mechanism](#the-mechanism)
+  - [Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)
+  - [Test Plan](#test-plan)
+      - [Prerequisite testing updates](#prerequisite-testing-updates)
+    - [Unit Tests](#unit-tests)
+    - [Integration tests](#integration-tests)
+  - [Graduation Criteria](#graduation-criteria)
+- [Implementation History](#implementation-history)
+- [Drawbacks](#drawbacks)
+- [Alternatives](#alternatives)
+    - [Delay job start instead of workload admission](#delay-job-start-instead-of-workload-admission)
+    - [Block job start only if two workloads overlap in flavors](#block-job-start-only-if-two-workloads-overlap-in-flavors)
+    - [Pod Resource Reservation](#pod-resource-reservation)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to ensure that a job gets the
+physical resources assigned once unsuspended by Kueue.
+
+<!--
+This section is incredibly important for producing high-quality, user-focused
+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be
+possible to collect this information before implementation begins, in order to
+avoid requiring implementors to split their attention between writing release
+notes and implementing the feature itself. KEP editors and SIG Docs
+should help to ensure that the tone and content of the `Summary` section is
+useful for a wide audience.
+
+A good summary is probably at least a paragraph in length.
+
+Both in this section and below, follow the guidelines of the [documentation
+style guide]. In particular, wrap lines to a reasonable length, to make it
+easier for reviewers to cite specific portions, and to minimize diff churn on
+updates.
+
+[documentation style guide]: https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/style-guide.md
+-->
+
+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to ensure that a job gets assigned physical resources when
+unsuspended by Kueue
+- a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady` condition by a workload since unsuspending
+of the corresponding Job
+
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to ensure workloads get their physical resources
+assigned by avoiding concurrent scheduling of workloads. More precisely, we
+block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the Job is scheduled.
+Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other admitted workloads
+have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by a Job by a new
+annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a workload, comprising tens of
+pods that all need to run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+cheduling of all pods in this workload take a couple of minutes and other
+workloads scheduled within this interval can occupy the resources my workload
+could use otherwise, potentially forcing additional scale-up events. I would
+like to be sure that my workload, once admitted, can get resources allocated to
+all pods without an unnecessary delay.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources a little higher than what is
+available in the quiet period. However, I don't want to risk two workloads
+deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these workloads don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+To mitigate this issue we introduce introduce a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)).
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate
+that the first batch of a workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
+
+The `PodsReady` condition is addded to the workload by the Kueue's Job
+reconciler in reaction to a status update of the corresponding Job. Note that,
+verifying if the condition should be added does not require an extra API call as
+the Kueue's Job reconciller already fetches the latest Job object at the
+beginning of the `Reconcile` function. Also note, there is no new condition
+introduced to the Job API.
+
+### Workload API
+
+We extend the Workload API with the new `startupRequirements` field to
+enable the new behavior.
+
+```golang
+// StartupRequirements describes workload startup requirements
+type StartupRequirements string
+
+const (
+  // QuotaAndPodsReady means that workload requires all pods to be running at
+  // the same time
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaAndPodsReady StartupRequirements = "QuotaAndPodsReady"
```

  Wouldn't actually be misleading, because `StartTime` in job points to the moment of unsuspending, whereas the `StartupRequirements` , but QuotaAndPodsReady value point to the moment when all pods are ready?

+

+

+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate

+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources a little higher than what is
+available in the quiet period. However, I don't want to risk two workloads
+deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these workloads don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+To mitigate this issue we introduce introduce a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)).
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate
+that the first batch of a workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
+
+The `PodsReady` condition is addded to the workload by the Kueue's Job
+reconciler in reaction to a status update of the corresponding Job. Note that,
+verifying if the condition should be added does not require an extra API call as
+the Kueue's Job reconciller already fetches the latest Job object at the
+beginning of the `Reconcile` function. Also note, there is no new condition
+introduced to the Job API.
+
+### Workload API
+
+We extend the Workload API with the new `startupRequirements` field to
+enable the new behavior.
+
+```golang
+// StartupRequirements describes workload startup requirements
+type StartupRequirements string
+
+const (
+  // QuotaAndPodsReady means that workload requires all pods to be running at
+  // the same time
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaAndPodsReady StartupRequirements = "QuotaAndPodsReady"
```

  It's about the requirements we have before starting the job. But sure, in the first implementation we will also block after we already started the Job.
  
  I don't have a strong opinion. Both `Start` and `Startup` are ok with me.

 introduced to the Job API.
 
+Note that, we don't take failed pods directly into the computation for verifying
+the `PodsReady` condition. However, we would eliminate a buggy workload based on
```

  It sounds like kueue will do something extra about this situation. But in reality we just wait for the job to fail.

@@ -0,0 +1,445 @@
+# KEP-349: All-or-nothing semantics for workload resource assignment
+
+<!--
+This is the title of your KEP. Keep it short, simple, and descriptive. A good
+title can help communicate what the KEP is and should be considered as part of
+any review.
+-->
+
+<!--
+A table of contents is helpful for quickly jumping to sections of a KEP and for
+highlighting any additional information provided beyond the standard KEP
+template.
+
+Ensure the TOC is wrapped with
+  <code>&lt;!-- toc --&rt;&lt;!-- /toc --&rt;</code>
+tags, and then generate with `hack/update-toc.sh`.
+-->
+
+<!-- toc -->
+- [Summary](#summary)
+- [Motivation](#motivation)
+  - [Goals](#goals)
+  - [Non-Goals](#non-goals)
+- [Proposal](#proposal)
+  - [User Stories (Optional)](#user-stories-optional)
+    - [Story 1](#story-1)
+    - [Story 2](#story-2)
+  - [Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)](#notescontraintscaveats-optional)
+  - [Risks and Mitigations](#risks-and-mitigations)
+- [Design Details](#design-details)
+  - [PodsReady workload condition](#podsready-workload-condition)
+  - [Workload API](#workload-api)
+  - [Job annotation](#job-annotation)
+  - [The mechanism](#the-mechanism)
+  - [Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)
+  - [Test Plan](#test-plan)
+      - [Prerequisite testing updates](#prerequisite-testing-updates)
+    - [Unit Tests](#unit-tests)
+    - [Integration tests](#integration-tests)
+  - [Graduation Criteria](#graduation-criteria)
+- [Implementation History](#implementation-history)
+- [Drawbacks](#drawbacks)
+- [Alternatives](#alternatives)
+    - [Delay job start instead of workload admission](#delay-job-start-instead-of-workload-admission)
+    - [Block job start only if two workloads overlap in flavors](#block-job-start-only-if-two-workloads-overlap-in-flavors)
+    - [Pod Resource Reservation](#pod-resource-reservation)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to ensure that a job gets the
+physical resources assigned once unsuspended by Kueue.
+
+<!--
+This section is incredibly important for producing high-quality, user-focused
+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be
+possible to collect this information before implementation begins, in order to
+avoid requiring implementors to split their attention between writing release
+notes and implementing the feature itself. KEP editors and SIG Docs
+should help to ensure that the tone and content of the `Summary` section is
+useful for a wide audience.
+
+A good summary is probably at least a paragraph in length.
+
+Both in this section and below, follow the guidelines of the [documentation
+style guide]. In particular, wrap lines to a reasonable length, to make it
+easier for reviewers to cite specific portions, and to minimize diff churn on
+updates.
+
+[documentation style guide]: https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/style-guide.md
+-->
+
+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to ensure that a job gets assigned physical resources when
+unsuspended by Kueue
+- a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady` condition by a workload since unsuspending
+of the corresponding Job
+
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to ensure workloads get their physical resources
+assigned by avoiding concurrent scheduling of workloads. More precisely, we
+block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the Job is scheduled.
+Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other admitted workloads
+have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by a Job by a new
+annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a workload, comprising tens of
+pods that all need to run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+cheduling of all pods in this workload take a couple of minutes and other
+workloads scheduled within this interval can occupy the resources my workload
+could use otherwise, potentially forcing additional scale-up events. I would
+like to be sure that my workload, once admitted, can get resources allocated to
+all pods without an unnecessary delay.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources a little higher than what is
+available in the quiet period. However, I don't want to risk two workloads
+deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these workloads don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+To mitigate this issue we introduce introduce a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)).
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate
+that the first batch of a workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
+
+The `PodsReady` condition is addded to the workload by the Kueue's Job
+reconciler in reaction to a status update of the corresponding Job. Note that,
+verifying if the condition should be added does not require an extra API call as
+the Kueue's Job reconciller already fetches the latest Job object at the
+beginning of the `Reconcile` function. Also note, there is no new condition
+introduced to the Job API.
+
+### Workload API
+
+We extend the Workload API with the new `startupRequirements` field to
+enable the new behavior.
+
+```golang
+// StartupRequirements describes workload startup requirements
+type StartupRequirements string
+
+const (
+  // QuotaAndPodsReady means that workload requires all pods to be running at
+  // the same time
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaAndPodsReady StartupRequirements = "QuotaAndPodsReady"
```

  I'm also ok with `Running`

+
+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to ensure that a job gets the
+physical resources assigned once unsuspended by Kueue.
+
+<!--
+This section is incredibly important for producing high-quality, user-focused
+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be
+possible to collect this information before implementation begins, in order to
+avoid requiring implementors to split their attention between writing release
+notes and implementing the feature itself. KEP editors and SIG Docs
+should help to ensure that the tone and content of the `Summary` section is
+useful for a wide audience.
+
+A good summary is probably at least a paragraph in length.
+
+Both in this section and below, follow the guidelines of the [documentation
+style guide]. In particular, wrap lines to a reasonable length, to make it
+easier for reviewers to cite specific portions, and to minimize diff churn on
+updates.
+
+[documentation style guide]: https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/style-guide.md
+-->
+
+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to ensure that a job gets assigned physical resources when
+unsuspended by Kueue
+- a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady` condition by a workload since unsuspending
+of the corresponding Job
+
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to ensure workloads get their physical resources
+assigned by avoiding concurrent scheduling of workloads. More precisely, we
+block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the Job is scheduled.
+Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other admitted workloads
+have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by a Job by a new
+annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a Job, comprising tens of
+pods that all need to run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+scheduling of all pods in this Job take a couple of minutes and pods created by
+other Jobs scheduled within this interval can occupy the resources my Job
+could use otherwise, potentially forcing additional scale-up events. I would
+like to be sure that my Job, once started, can get resources allocated to
+all pods without an unnecessary delay.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources. However, I don't want to risk
+two Jobs deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+cluster queue quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these Jobs don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+#### Choosing the API object to opt-in for the mechanism
+
+Let's review the options for the best place for the API and their pros&cons:
+- job (current proposal)
+- local queue
+- cluster queue
+- component
+
+**Workload/Job level pros**:
+- throughput - the same cluster queue may run workloads which don't need any guarantees and thus get admitted faster.
+- flexibility -  we can easily implement queue-level (or component-level) by translating the queue-level property into workload property during workload creation. For example, add the workload annotation to every workload created for a given local queue.
+- conceptually, requiring all pods to run at the same time is a property of a workload itself.
+
+**Workload/Job level cons**:
+- introducing a new annotation on a Job level will increase the complexity of the job reconciler which is a reference Job-based implementation of CRD <> Workload integration. Similarly, introducing
+  a timeout on reaching `PodsReady` condition could increase the complexity of the reference implementation.
+
+**Local Queue level pros**:
+- does not increase complexity of the Job reconciler which is the reference implementation for the CRD - Workload integration in case of Job,
+- allows for the same throughput as workload level if Jobs that don't require the `PodsReady` condition are sent to another LocalQueue
+- the workload-level field could be still populated based on the assigned local queue
+- could be a natual place to also define timeout for reaching `PodsReady`, which is not component-level
+
+**Local Queue level cons**:
+- similar complexity of implementation comparing to workload level - in order to ensure exclusive start of pods we still need to both block admission of new workloads until in `PodsReady` and await for all already workloads to be in the `PodsReady` condition, as some workloads might be executed on the cluster bypassing the queues.
+- throughput - it could be compensated by creating dedicated queues for workloads requiring all-or-nothing or not requiring it.
```

  > this is not necessarily true, because once a workload that requires all-or-nothing is being admitted, we wait for all workloads in the cluster, don't we?
  
  indeed, removed the misleading point.

+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to ensure that a job gets the
+physical resources assigned once unsuspended by Kueue.
+

+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be

+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to ensure that a job gets assigned physical resources when
+unsuspended by Kueue
+- a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady` condition by a workload since unsuspending
+of the corresponding Job
+
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to ensure workloads get their physical resources
+assigned by avoiding concurrent scheduling of workloads. More precisely, we
+block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the Job is scheduled.
+Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other admitted workloads
+have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by a Job by a new
+annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a Job, comprising tens of
+pods that all need to run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+scheduling of all pods in this Job take a couple of minutes and pods created by
+other Jobs scheduled within this interval can occupy the resources my Job
+could use otherwise, potentially forcing additional scale-up events. I would
+like to be sure that my Job, once started, can get resources allocated to
+all pods without an unnecessary delay.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources. However, I don't want to risk
+two Jobs deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+cluster queue quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these Jobs don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+#### Choosing the API object to opt-in for the mechanism
+
+Let's review the options for the best place for the API and their pros&cons:
+- job (current proposal)
+- local queue
+- cluster queue
+- component
+
+**Workload/Job level pros**:
+- throughput - the same cluster queue may run workloads which don't need any guarantees and thus get admitted faster.
+- flexibility -  we can easily implement queue-level (or component-level) by translating the queue-level property into workload property during workload creation. For example, add the workload annotation to every workload created for a given local queue.
+- conceptually, requiring all pods to run at the same time is a property of a workload itself.
+
+**Workload/Job level cons**:
+- introducing a new annotation on a Job level will increase the complexity of the job reconciler which is a reference Job-based implementation of CRD <> Workload integration. Similarly, introducing
+  a timeout on reaching `PodsReady` condition could increase the complexity of the reference implementation.
+
+**Local Queue level pros**:
+- does not increase complexity of the Job reconciler which is the reference implementation for the CRD - Workload integration in case of Job,
+- allows for the same throughput as workload level if Jobs that don't require the `PodsReady` condition are sent to another LocalQueue
+- the workload-level field could be still populated based on the assigned local queue
+- could be a natual place to also define timeout for reaching `PodsReady`, which is not component-level
+
+**Local Queue level cons**:
+- similar complexity of implementation comparing to workload level - in order to ensure exclusive start of pods we still need to both block admission of new workloads until in `PodsReady` and await for all already workloads to be in the `PodsReady` condition, as some workloads might be executed on the cluster bypassing the queues.
+
+**Cluster Queue level cons**:
+- cluster queue is only assigned after admission so it blocks us the ability to block admission for a given workload
+- otherwise, same cons as for Local Queue.
+
+**Component level pros**:
+- lower complexity of implementation comparing to workload level - we could just block admission until the workload is in the `PodsScheduled` state. We wouldn't need to check if there are any already admitted workloads - we know they all have already awaited to be in the `PodsScheduled` condition.
+
+**Component level cons**:
+- throughput - would degrade substantially by sequencing of all workloads, even those which don't require the semantics
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+To mitigate this issue we introduce a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)).
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate
+that the first batch of a workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
+
+The `PodsReady` condition is addded to the workload by the Kueue's Job
+reconciler in reaction to a status update of the corresponding Job. Note that,
+verifying if the condition should be added does not require an extra API call as
+the Kueue's Job reconciller already fetches the latest Job object at the
+beginning of the `Reconcile` function. Also note, there is no new condition
+introduced to the Job API.
+
+Note that, we don't take failed pods directly into the computation for verifying
+the `PodsReady` condition. However, a buggy workload is eliminated as the
+correspoding job fails due to exceeding the `.spec.backoffLimit` limit.
+
+### Workload API
+
+We extend the Workload API with the new `startupRequirements` field to
+enable the new behavior.
+
+```golang
+// StartupRequirements describes workload startup requirements
+type StartupRequirements string
+
+const (
+  // QuotaAndPodsReady: means that workload needs to get the ClusterQueue
+  // quota to be admitted and to have all its pods running at the same time
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaAndPodsReady StartupRequirements = "QuotaAndPodsReady"
+  // QuotaOnly means that the only requirement for a workload to start is to
+  // get resources from the ClusterQueue quota
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaOnly StartupRequirements = "QuotaOnly"
```

  With current naming, I find the API suspicious conceptually. I think we would rather want to assume that all jobs want atomic start. Or at least we already assume that with quota. Consider that QuotaOnly is artificial -- if the job is okay with partial start, why wouldn't quota allow that? One day we might want a true option for partial start (e.g. # min pods to start), but quota should respect it too.
  
  So I think it's not really a Job option, but a temporary tradeoff in Kueue -- we'd like to help jobs with atomic expectations (all jobs with current Kueue assumptions), but the only way for now is to slow down to sequential admission. Since it's slower, we offer it as an option, e.g. ensure-pods-start=true. Once we have the efficient parallel solution for atomic provisioning (Resource Reservation), we would likely want to make it default. In any case, I don't see much space conceptually for QuotaOnly in between partial and atomic jobs.

+

+    - [Delay job start instead of workload admission](#delay-job-start-instead-of-workload-admission)
+    - [Block job start only if two workloads overlap in flavors](#block-job-start-only-if-two-workloads-overlap-in-flavors)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to avoid concurrent scheduling of
+pods from different workloads, if they require all-or-nothing scheduling
+guarantees.
+
+<!--
+This section is incredibly important for producing high-quality, user-focused
+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be
+possible to collect this information before implementation begins, in order to
+avoid requiring implementors to split their attention between writing release
+notes and implementing the feature itself. KEP editors and SIG Docs
+should help to ensure that the tone and content of the `Summary` section is
+useful for a wide audience.
+
+A good summary is probably at least a paragraph in length.
+
+Both in this section and below, follow the guidelines of the [documentation
+style guide]. In particular, wrap lines to a reasonable length, to make it
+easier for reviewers to cite specific portions, and to minimize diff churn on
+updates.
+
+[documentation style guide]: https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/style-guide.md
+-->
+
+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to avoid concurrent scheduling of pods for workloads.
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to avoid concurrent scheduling of Job pods. More
+precisely, we block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the
+Job is scheduled. Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other
+admitted workloads have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by
+a Job by a new annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a workload, comprising tens of
+pods which need to all run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+scheduling of all pods in this workload take a couple of minutes and I want to
+have a guarantee that other workloads scheduled within this time interval would
+not delay execution of my workload.
+
+My use can be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/wait-for-pods-scheduled`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources a little higher than what is
+available in the quiet period. However, I don't want to risk two workloads
+deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+quota.
+
+My use can be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/wait-for-pods-scheduled`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+The risk can be mitigated by using the Job's `ActiveDeadlineSeconds` timeout
+to terminate a started job, which results in finishing the associated workload.
+
+Still the mitigation might be problematic as the timeout needs to include both
+the scheduling and execution time for a job, thus needs to be higher than one
+would want.
+
+Thus, we consider introduction of timeout on reaching the `PodsScheduled`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsScheduled condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsscheduled-condition))
+as a possible future improvement.
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsScheduled workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsScheduled`, to indicate
+that the first batch of workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
```

  We have a long term plan to use LocalQueue as a place for workload defaulting, I wouldn't add it now though, lets give the feature time to soak a bit, so perhaps starting with an annotation on the Job API is a good first step.

+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to ensure that a job gets the
+physical resources assigned once unsuspended by Kueue.

+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.

+- a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady` condition by a workload since unsuspending
+of the corresponding Job

+We introduce a mechanism to ensure workloads get their physical resources
+assigned by avoiding concurrent scheduling of workloads. More precisely, we
+block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the Job is scheduled.
+Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other admitted workloads
+have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by a Job by a new
+annotation.
+

+

+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a workload, comprising tens of
+pods that all need to run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+cheduling of all pods in this workload take a couple of minutes and other
+workloads scheduled within this interval can occupy the resources my workload
+could use otherwise, potentially forcing additional scale-up events. I would
+like to be sure that my workload, once admitted, can get resources allocated to
+all pods without an unnecessary delay.
+
+My use can be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/workload-startup-requirements: QuotaAndProdsReady`

+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able

+quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these workloads don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use can be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/workload-startup-requirements: QuotaAndProdsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.

+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+To mitigate this issue we introduce introduce a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)).

+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate
+that the first batch of a workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
+
+The `PodsReady` condition is addded to the workload by the Kueue's Job
+reconciler in reaction to a status update of the corresponding Job. Note that,
+verifying if the condition should be added does not require an extra API call as
+the Kueue's Job reconciller already fetches the latest Job object at the
+beginning of the `Reconcile` function. Also note, there is no new condition
+introduced to the Job API.
+
+### Workload API
+
+We extend the Workload API with the new `startupRequirements` field to
+enable the new behavior.
+
+```golang
+// StartupRequirements describes workload startup requirements
+type StartupRequirements string
+
+const (
+  // QuotaAndProdsReady means that workload requires all pods to be running at
+  // the same time
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaAndProdsReady StartupRequirements = "QuotaAndProdsReady"
+  // QuotaOnly means that the only requirement for a workload to start is to
+  // get resources from the cluster quota
```

  hmm, I think the context here is clear, quota in Kueue docs refers to Kueue-managed quota in ClusterQueues.

### Review by [@mimowo](https://github.com/mimowo) (commented) — 2022-11-29T17:16:24Z

- **keps/349-avoid-coscheduling/README.md:211** by [@mimowo](https://github.com/mimowo) — 2022-11-29T17:16:24Z
```diff
@@ -0,0 +1,418 @@
+# KEP-349: Avoid concurrent pod scheduling for workloads
+
+<!--
+This is the title of your KEP. Keep it short, simple, and descriptive. A good
+title can help communicate what the KEP is and should be considered as part of
+any review.
+-->
+
+<!--
+A table of contents is helpful for quickly jumping to sections of a KEP and for
+highlighting any additional information provided beyond the standard KEP
+template.
+
+Ensure the TOC is wrapped with
+  <code>&lt;!-- toc --&rt;&lt;!-- /toc --&rt;</code>
+tags, and then generate with `hack/update-toc.sh`.
+-->
+
+<!-- toc -->
+- [Summary](#summary)
+- [Motivation](#motivation)
+  - [Goals](#goals)
+  - [Non-Goals](#non-goals)
+- [Proposal](#proposal)
+  - [User Stories (Optional)](#user-stories-optional)
+    - [Story 1](#story-1)
+    - [Story 2](#story-2)
+  - [Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)](#notescontraintscaveats-optional)
+  - [Risks and Mitigations](#risks-and-mitigations)
+- [Design Details](#design-details)
+  - [PodsScheduled workload condition](#podsscheduled-workload-condition)
+  - [Workload API](#workload-api)
+  - [Job annotation](#job-annotation)
+  - [The mechanism](#the-mechanism)
+  - [Test Plan](#test-plan)
+      - [Prerequisite testing updates](#prerequisite-testing-updates)
+    - [Unit Tests](#unit-tests)
+    - [Integration tests](#integration-tests)
+  - [Graduation Criteria](#graduation-criteria)
+- [Implementation History](#implementation-history)
+- [Drawbacks](#drawbacks)
+- [Alternatives](#alternatives)
+    - [Timeout on reaching the PodsScheduled condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsscheduled-condition)
+    - [Delay job start instead of workload admission](#delay-job-start-instead-of-workload-admission)
+    - [Block job start only if two workloads overlap in flavors](#block-job-start-only-if-two-workloads-overlap-in-flavors)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to avoid concurrent scheduling of
+pods from different workloads, if they require all-or-nothing scheduling
+guarantees.
+
+<!--
+This section is incredibly important for producing high-quality, user-focused
+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be
+possible to collect this information before implementation begins, in order to
+avoid requiring implementors to split their attention between writing release
+notes and implementing the feature itself. KEP editors and SIG Docs
+should help to ensure that the tone and content of the `Summary` section is
+useful for a wide audience.
+
+A good summary is probably at least a paragraph in length.
+
+Both in this section and below, follow the guidelines of the [documentation
+style guide]. In particular, wrap lines to a reasonable length, to make it
+easier for reviewers to cite specific portions, and to minimize diff churn on
+updates.
+
+[documentation style guide]: https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/style-guide.md
+-->
+
+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to avoid concurrent scheduling of pods for workloads.
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to avoid concurrent scheduling of Job pods. More
+precisely, we block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the
+Job is scheduled. Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other
+admitted workloads have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by
+a Job by a new annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a workload, comprising tens of
+pods which need to all run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+scheduling of all pods in this workload take a couple of minutes and I want to
+have a guarantee that other workloads scheduled within this time interval would
+not delay execution of my workload.
+
+My use can be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/wait-for-pods-scheduled`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources a little higher than what is
+available in the quiet period. However, I don't want to risk two workloads
+deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+quota.
+
+My use can be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/wait-for-pods-scheduled`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+The risk can be mitigated by using the Job's `ActiveDeadlineSeconds` timeout
+to terminate a started job, which results in finishing the associated workload.
+
+Still the mitigation might be problematic as the timeout needs to include both
+the scheduling and execution time for a job, thus needs to be higher than one
+would want.
+
+Thus, we consider introduction of timeout on reaching the `PodsScheduled`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsScheduled condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsscheduled-condition))
+as a possible future improvement.
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsScheduled workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsScheduled`, to indicate
+that the first batch of workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
```

  There are a couple of advantages which LocalQueue seems to have:
  - it does not leak implementation to JobReconciler, allowing to keep it lean as a reference implementation of the CRD <> Workload integration (https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kueue/pull/433#discussion_r1034128187). 
  - it would also be a natural place for the timeout, currently the opt-in and related timeout are on two opposite ends of the concepts. Then, it allows you to have two LocalQueues, so that the timeouts could be different for large and small jobs.
  
  In terms of implementation complexity, I think it is similar, but we have already a webhook for validating LocalQueue, and if we want to validate the job annotation we would need to introduce a new one. 
  
  I also think that in terms of the "first step" it also seems more natural to stay within Kueue concepts.

@@ -0,0 +1,485 @@
+# KEP-349: All-or-nothing semantics for workload resource assignment
+
+<!--
+This is the title of your KEP. Keep it short, simple, and descriptive. A good
+title can help communicate what the KEP is and should be considered as part of
+any review.
+-->
+
+<!--

+highlighting any additional information provided beyond the standard KEP
+template.
+
+Ensure the TOC is wrapped with
+  <code>&lt;!-- toc --&rt;&lt;!-- /toc --&rt;</code>
+tags, and then generate with `hack/update-toc.sh`.
+-->
+

+    - [Delay job start instead of workload admission](#delay-job-start-instead-of-workload-admission)
+    - [Block job start only if two workloads overlap in flavors](#block-job-start-only-if-two-workloads-overlap-in-flavors)
+    - [Pod Resource Reservation](#pod-resource-reservation)
+<!-- /toc -->
+
+## Summary
+
+This proposal introduces an opt-in mechanism to ensure that a job gets the
+physical resources assigned once unsuspended by Kueue.
+
+<!--
+This section is incredibly important for producing high-quality, user-focused
+documentation such as release notes or a development roadmap. It should be
+possible to collect this information before implementation begins, in order to
+avoid requiring implementors to split their attention between writing release
+notes and implementing the feature itself. KEP editors and SIG Docs
+should help to ensure that the tone and content of the `Summary` section is
+useful for a wide audience.
+
+A good summary is probably at least a paragraph in length.
+
+Both in this section and below, follow the guidelines of the [documentation
+style guide]. In particular, wrap lines to a reasonable length, to make it
+easier for reviewers to cite specific portions, and to minimize diff churn on
+updates.
+
+[documentation style guide]: https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/style-guide.md
+-->
+
+## Motivation
+
+Some workloads need all pods to be running at the same time to make progress, for
+example, when they require pod-to-pod communication. In that case a large
+workload may have difficulty scheduling all of its pods for a prolonged time as
+smaller workloads would continue to block the slots. Moreover, a pair of
+workloads may deadlock if the amount of actual resources available is below the
+quota configured to determine admission.
+
+<!--
+This section is for explicitly listing the motivation, goals, and non-goals of
+this KEP.  Describe why the change is important and the benefits to users. The
+motivation section can optionally provide links to [experience reports] to
+demonstrate the interest in a KEP within the wider Kubernetes community.
+
+[experience reports]: https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/ExperienceReports
+-->
+
+### Goals
+
+- a mechanism to ensure that a job gets assigned physical resources when
+unsuspended by Kueue
+- a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady` condition by a workload since unsuspending
+of the corresponding Job
+
+<!--
+List the specific goals of the KEP. What is it trying to achieve? How will we
+know that this has succeeded?
+-->
+
+### Non-Goals
+
+- guarantee that two workloads would not schedule pods concurrently. Example
+scenarios in which two workloads may still concurrently schedule their pods:
+  - when succeeded pods are replaced with new because job's parallelism is less than its completions;
+  - when a failed pod gets replaced
+
+<!--
+What is out of scope for this KEP? Listing non-goals helps to focus discussion
+and make progress.
+-->
+
+## Proposal
+
+We introduce a mechanism to ensure workloads get their physical resources
+assigned by avoiding concurrent scheduling of workloads. More precisely, we
+block admission of new workloads until the first batch of the Job is scheduled.
+Additionally, we await with starting the Job until all other admitted workloads
+have their pods scheduled. This behavior can be opted-in by a Job by a new
+annotation.
+
+<!--
+This is where we get down to the specifics of what the proposal actually is.
+This should have enough detail that reviewers can understand exactly what
+you're proposing, but should not include things like API designs or
+implementation. What is the desired outcome and how do we measure success?.
+The "Design Details" section below is for the real
+nitty-gritty.
+-->
+
+### User Stories (Optional)
+
+<!--
+Detail the things that people will be able to do if this KEP is implemented.
+Include as much detail as possible so that people can understand the "how" of
+the system. The goal here is to make this feel real for users without getting
+bogged down.
+-->
+
+#### Story 1
+
+As a machine learning researcher I want to run a Job, comprising tens of
+pods that all need to run at the same time. Autoscaling the cluster and
+scheduling of all pods in this Job take a couple of minutes and pods created by
+other Jobs scheduled within this interval can occupy the resources my Job
+could use otherwise, potentially forcing additional scale-up events. I would
+like to be sure that my Job, once started, can get resources allocated to
+all pods without an unnecessary delay.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to the jobs I run.
+
+#### Story 2
+
+As an administrator of a batch processing in my organization I want to be able
+to configure kueue quotas for on-demand resources. However, I don't want to risk
+two Jobs deadlock in case of issues with provisioning nodes to satisfy the configured
+cluster queue quota. This could happen when the job corresponding to these Jobs don't
+specify priorities or specify the same priority.
+
+My use case be supported by adding `kueue.x-k8s.io/startup-requirements: QuotaAndPodsReady`
+annotation to all jobs.
+
+### Notes/Contraints/Caveats (Optional)
+
+<!--
+What are the caveats to the proposal?
+What are some important details that didn't come across above?
+Go in to as much detail as necessary here.
+This might be a good place to talk about core concepts and how they relate.
+-->
+
+#### Choosing the API object to opt-in for the mechanism
+
+Let's review the options for the best place for the API and their pros&cons:
+- job (current proposal)
+- local queue
+- cluster queue
+- component
+
+**Workload/Job level pros**:
+- throughput - the same cluster queue may run workloads which don't need any guarantees and thus get admitted faster.
+- flexibility -  we can easily implement queue-level (or component-level) by translating the queue-level property into workload property during workload creation. For example, add the workload annotation to every workload created for a given local queue.
+- conceptually, requiring all pods to run at the same time is a property of a workload itself.
+
+**Workload/Job level cons**:
+- introducing a new annotation on a Job level will increase the complexity of the job reconciler which is a reference Job-based implementation of CRD <> Workload integration. Similarly, introducing
+  a timeout on reaching `PodsReady` condition could increase the complexity of the reference implementation.
+
+**Local Queue level pros**:
+- does not increase complexity of the Job reconciler which is the reference implementation for the CRD - Workload integration in case of Job,
+- allows for the same throughput as workload level if Jobs that don't require the `PodsReady` condition are sent to another LocalQueue
+- the workload-level field could be still populated based on the assigned local queue
+- could be a natual place to also define timeout for reaching `PodsReady`, which is not component-level
+
+**Local Queue level cons**:
+- similar complexity of implementation comparing to workload level - in order to ensure exclusive start of pods we still need to both block admission of new workloads until in `PodsReady` and await for all already workloads to be in the `PodsReady` condition, as some workloads might be executed on the cluster bypassing the queues.
+
+**Cluster Queue level cons**:
+- cluster queue is only assigned after admission so it blocks us the ability to block admission for a given workload
+- otherwise, same cons as for Local Queue.
+
+**Component level pros**:
+- lower complexity of implementation comparing to workload level - we could just block admission until the workload is in the `PodsScheduled` state. We wouldn't need to check if there are any already admitted workloads - we know they all have already awaited to be in the `PodsScheduled` condition.
+
+**Component level cons**:
+- throughput - would degrade substantially by sequencing of all workloads, even those which don't require the semantics
+
+### Risks and Mitigations
+
+If the workload fails to schedule its pods it could block admission of other
+workloads indefinitely.
+
+To mitigate this issue we introduce a timeout on reaching the `PodsReady`
+condition by a workload since its job start (see:
+[Timeout on reaching the PodsReady condition](#timeout-on-reaching-the-podsready-condition)).
+
+<!--
+What are the risks of this proposal, and how do we mitigate? Think broadly.
+For example, consider both security and how this will impact the larger
+Kubernetes ecosystem.
+
+How will security be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+How will UX be reviewed, and by whom?
+
+Consider including folks who also work outside the SIG or subproject.
+-->
+
+## Design Details
+
+<!--
+This section should contain enough information that the specifics of your
+change are understandable. This may include API specs (though not always
+required) or even code snippets. If there's any ambiguity about HOW your
+proposal will be implemented, this is the place to discuss them.
+-->
+
+
+### PodsReady workload condition
+
+First, we introduce a new workload condition, called `PodsReady`, to indicate
+that the first batch of a workload's pods is scheduled, more precisely:
+`job.status.ready + job.status.succeeded >= job.spec.parallelism`.
+
+The `PodsReady` condition is addded to the workload by the Kueue's Job
+reconciler in reaction to a status update of the corresponding Job. Note that,
+verifying if the condition should be added does not require an extra API call as
+the Kueue's Job reconciller already fetches the latest Job object at the
+beginning of the `Reconcile` function. Also note, there is no new condition
+introduced to the Job API.
+
+Note that, we don't take failed pods directly into the computation for verifying
+the `PodsReady` condition. However, a buggy workload is eliminated as the
+correspoding job fails due to exceeding the `.spec.backoffLimit` limit.
+
+### Workload API
+
+We extend the Workload API with the new `startupRequirements` field to
+enable the new behavior.
+
+```golang
+// StartupRequirements describes workload startup requirements
+type StartupRequirements string
+
+const (
+  // QuotaAndPodsReady: means that workload needs to get the ClusterQueue
+  // quota to be admitted and to have all its pods running at the same time
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaAndPodsReady StartupRequirements = "QuotaAndPodsReady"
+  // QuotaOnly means that the only requirement for a workload to start is to
+  // get resources from the ClusterQueue quota
+  StartupRequirementsQuotaOnly StartupRequirements = "QuotaOnly"
```

  I am on the fence on this as well, I made a similar comment on the initial proposal. The relevant workload/job API is whether or not the job wants all-or-nothing scheduling. 
  
  A global option is going to be too strict. Consider a cluster with two types of resources: reservations and spot. Jobs admitted on reservations don't need to ensure that pods are ready, while ones admitted on spot will want to due to the potential of stockouts. This issue however will not be solved with the current workload-level API (because CQ could have multiple flavors that a workload can fallback to), it would be solved if the API is part of the resourceFlavor one.
  
  That being said, I think it is safer to start with a global option, it will not be too hard to extend it to either a workload-level API or a resourceFlavor one if we needed that and can't wait for the CA provisioning API.

### Review by [@ahg-g](https://github.com/ahg-g) (commented) — 2022-11-29T17:26:30Z
